label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. So, the story is about Mr. Williams who was driving and encountered a "SLOW" sign at a crossroad. He slowed down, checked both ways, and then drove across without stopping. Then a policeman stopped him, saying he didn't stop at the crossing. Mr. Williams explained that the sign said "SLOW," not "STOP," and he did slow down. The policeman then realized he was in the wrong street. The question is asking what Mr. Williams was doing when he was stopped by the policeman. The options are: A) was driving at a high speed B) was driving onto the main road C) was going to stop his car D) was driving slowly Let me recall the details. The key part is when Mr. Williams approached the crossroad with a "SLOW" sign. He slowed down, looked both ways, and then drove across without stopping. So when he was stopped by the policeman, he was in the process of driving across the main road, having already slowed down. The policeman's issue was that he didn't stop, but the sign only said "SLOW," so Mr. Williams was correct in slowing but not stopping. However, the policeman realized his mistake and said he was in the wrong street. So the question is about what he was doing at the moment of being stopped. The options are about his actions. Let's look at each option: A) High speed: But he slowed down, so this is incorrect. B) Driving onto the main road: The crossroad had a "SLOW" sign, meaning he was on a secondary road approaching the main road. He drove across (onto the main road?) Wait, maybe he was on the main road? Wait, the first paragraph says that at some crossroads, you have to stop when coming to the main road. Other crossroads have "SLOW," meaning you slow but don't stop unless something is coming. At others, you don't need to slow or stop because you're on the main road. So Mr. Williams was at a crossroad with a "SLOW" sign. That implies that he was on a minor road approaching the main road. The "SLOW" sign would require him to slow down before crossing the main road, but not to stop unless necessary. So when he drove across, he was driving onto the main road. Then he was stopped by the policeman.
